Nephew of Aisha and primary transmitter of her accounts with direct access to her knowledge

Ali succeeded Uthman after his assassination
Uthman succeeded Umar as third Caliph but faced rebellion that led to his assassination.
Uthman married Fatimah's daughter Ruqayyah, then after her death, married Umm Kulthum
Both were scholars preserving and transmitting Islamic and historical knowledge during Umayyad-to-Abbasid transition
Wahshi was slave of Jubayr. Hind promised freedom for killing Hamza. The obligation was fulfilled — the redemption came later.
Waki's standards for hadith transmission influenced Abu Dawud's rigorous compilation methodology
Distinguished hadith scholar and teacher of Ahmad ibn Hanbal
Waki represented orthodox hadith scholarship; Ahmad pursued theological innovation through Mu'tazila

Yunus part of generation learning from established scholars like Zuhrah
Both hadith narrators spanning Umayyad-Abbasid transition preserving Islamic knowledge
Zahdam (640-730 CE) represents Tabi'in while Muslim ibn Sa'id represents the Tabi' al-Tabi'in succession

related_name: Said ibn Zaid ibn Amr ibn Nufail
notes: Said ibn Zaid was the son of Zaid ibn Amr ibn Nufail; father was pre-Islamic monotheist seeker, son became prominent Sahabi
Pre-Islamic monotheistic seeker whose spiritual path was completed in the Abrahamic continuity that Abdullah ibn Salam represented

Freed slave of Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab who became respected teacher in Medina
Father of Usamah ibn Zayd; Zayd was adoptive son of Prophet Muhammad
Daughter of Ali, witnessed Karbala, spoke to power
Zaynab was formerly married to Zaid ibn Haritha (Usama's father) before marrying the Prophet, transcending the adoption system

Zirr as early Tabi'i represents the tradition Nubaih and contemporaries continued as hadith narrators

Ziyad ibn Jubair representing earlier generation of Tabi' al-Tabi'in scholars
Second generation of Islamic scholars transmitting to master hadith scholar
Wife and queen consort; wielded significant power in own right

Younger brother who succeeded Saladin and sustained the Ayyubid dynasty
Al-Adil as Ayyubid military commander opposed Crusader forces under Conrad's command

Galen built upon and extended Aristotelian natural philosophy in medical practice and theory
